questions.text: Myelin sheath is produced by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Astrocytes donot form myelin sheath.
Osteoclasts are bone destroying cells, hence they donot form myelin sheath.
Osteoclasts are bone destroying cells and astrocytes are subtype of glial cells , they donot form myelin sheath.
Schwann cells and oligodendrocytes form myelin sheath around the axon. Myelin sheath serves as an insulating layer, preventing loss of energy of the nerve impulse during its passage along the fibre.

questions.explanation: Schwann cells and oligodendrocytes form myelin sheath around the axon. Myelin sheath serves as an insulating layer, preventing loss of energy of the nerve impulse during its passage along the fibre.

questions.text: Receptor sites for neurotransmitters are present on the:

options.explanation: Presynaptic membrane has synaptic vesicles which contain neurotransmitter. When nerve impulse reaches the vesicles, neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft.
Tips of axons have synaptic vesicles, which contain neurotransmitter.

A neurotransmitter is a chemical substance responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse. It is released by the synaptic vesicle into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds with protein receptor molecule present on the postsynaptic membrane causing its depolarization and generation of an action potential.
Synaptic vesicles contain neurotransmitter for transmission of impulse.

questions.text: Which of the following regions of the brain is incorrectly paired with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Corpus callosum -­ communication between the left and right cerebral cortices
options.explanation: Activity of two hemispheres is coordinated by corpus callosum.
Cerebrum is involved in intelligence, memory,reasoning,thoughts and judgements.
Medulla oblongata has homeostatic control as it has reflex center for vital tasks like heart beat, breathing ,blood,pressure,coughing,sneezing,swallowing,vomiting,digestion and hiccuping.

Language comprehension is a function of the cerebrum. Cerebellum coordinates and controls rapid muscular activities such as running, typing etc. Although it does not initiate such voluntary movements, it is an important centre for coordinating movements and for controlling posture and balance. Cerebellum’s function is almost exclusively motor; but it is also implicated in some forms of learning.

questions.text: Injury localized to the hypothalamus would most likely disrupt:

options.explanation: Frontal cortex is involved in short term memory.
This is the function of cerebellum.

options.text: Executive functions; such as decision making
options.explanation: Executive functions like decision making is the role of cortex.

Hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus.It provides anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems by its relationship to the pituitary gland. Hypothalamus is thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt regulation of body temperature.

questions.text: The human hind brain comprises three parts one of which is the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spinal cord lies below medulla and extends down through neural canal of vertebrae upto the hips.
Activity of two cerebral hemispheres is coordinated by corpus callosum.

The brain is the anterior-most part of the central nervous system. The human brain can be divided into three parts: forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain. The human hindbrain comprises pons, cerebellum and medulla (also called the medulla oblongata).
It is the part of forebrain.

questions.explanation: The brain is the anterior-most part of the central nervous system. The human brain can be divided into three parts: forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain. The human hindbrain comprises pons, cerebellum and medulla (also called the medulla oblongata).

questions.text: Which part of human brain is concerned with the regulation of body temperature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Your cerebellum is a part of your brain located at the back of your head, just above and behind where your spinal cord connects to your brain itself. It is involved in muscular coordination,balance and positio of body in space.

Cerebrum, the largest and uppermost portion of the brain. The cerebrum consists of the cerebral hemispheres and accounts for two-thirds of the total weight of the brain. One hemisphere, usually the left, is functionally dominant, controlling language and speech.
Your hypothalamus, a structure deep in your brain, acts as your body's smart control coordinating center. Its main function is to keep your body in a stable state called homeostasis.
Medulla oblongata is the reflex center of vital tasks like heart beat,breathing,sneezing,vomiting and coughing.

options.text: First positive then negative and continues to be negative
First negative then positive and continues to be positive
First positive then negative and again back to positive

options.explanation: A nerve is a strand of tissue comprising many nerve fibres plus supporting tissue enclosed in a connective tissue sheath. The nerve impulse is the signal that travels along the length of a nerve fibre. This is how information is transmitted through the nervous system. It is marked by the flow of ions across the membrane of the axon caused by changes in the permeability of the membrane, producing a reduction in the potential difference that can be detected as the action potential. The strength of the impulse produced in any nerve fibre is constant.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not act as a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisone is a pregnene steroid hormone. It is a naturally-occurring corticosteroid metabolite that is also used as a pharmaceutical prodrug.

Acetylcholine is an organic compound that functions in the brain and body of many types of animals as a neurotransmitter. Its name is derived from its chemical structure: it is an ester of acetic acid and choline. Parts in the body that use or are affected by acetylcholine are referred to as cholinergic.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.
Norepinephrine, also called noradrenaline or noradrenalin, is an organic chemical in the catecholamine family that functions in the brain and body as both a hormone and neurotransmitter.

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemicals that are used to relay, amplify and modulate electrical signals between a neuron and another cell. Substances that act as neurotransmitters can be categorized into three major groups: (1) amino acids (primarily glutamic acid, GABA, aspartic acid and glycine), (2) peptides (vasopressin, somatostatin, neurotensin, etc.), and (3) monoamines (norepinephrine, dopamine and serotonin) plus acetylcholine. Cortisone is a glucocorticoid steroid hormone, produced by the adrenal glands and has anti-inflammatory and immune system suppressing properties.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct for the the node of Ranvier of the nerve?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nuerolemma is the membrane of neuron. It is not discontinuous.

At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

options.text: Both neurilemma and myelin sheath are discontinuous.
options.explanation: Both of them are not discontinuous.

options.text: It is covered by the myelin sheath
options.explanation: It is not totally covered by myelin sheath

Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

questions.text: The junction between the axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the next is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect.
Junction is a meeting point.
A joint is where two bones join together.

Consecutive neurons are so arranged that the axon endings of one neuron are connected to the dendrites of the next neuron. There is no cytoplasmic connection between the two neurons and microscopic gaps are left between them. Each of these contact points is known as synapse.

questions.explanation: Synapse is the close proximity of the axon of one neuron and the dendrite or cyton of another neuron with a gap of just about 200 Å in between. A nerve impulse is transmitted across the synapse by the release from the presynaptic membrane of neurotransmitter, which diffuses across the synaptic cleft to the post synaptic membrane. This triggers the propagation of the impulse from the dendrite along the length of the post synaptic neuron.

questions.text: Which of the following is regarded as a unit of nervous tissue?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neurons or nerve cells are the structural and functional units of the nervous system. These have a special structure but vary greatly in size and shape. Each neuron has a cell body which encloses cytoplasm and has a nucleus. A number of processes arise from the cell body. There is usually a single axon and a variable number of dendrites. The medullated nerve fibres are composed of a shining, white, fatty substance called myelin.
The myelin sheath is a greatly extended and modified plasma membrane wrapped around the nerve axon in a spiral fashion.
Axons are the elongated portion of the neurone located in the centre of the cell between the soma and axon terminals.

A dendrite (tree branch) is where a neuron receives input from other cells. Dendrites branch as they move towards their tips, just like tree branches do, and they even have leaf-like structures on them called spines.

questions.text: One function of the parasympathetic nervous system is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The contraction of hair muscles, also known as arrector pili muscles, is primarily regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. It causes hair to stand on end, a response known as piloerection.

Sweat gland activity is mainly regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic system promotes the production and release of sweat in response to heat or stress, not the parasympathetic system.
The acceleration of the heart rate is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. It triggers the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate to prepare the body for action.

This is the correct answer. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for constricting the pupil (making it smaller) in response to non-stressful, restful situations. This action allows for better near vision and reduces the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.explanation: The action of the parasympathetic nervous system is opposite to that of the sympathetic nervous system. If the sympathetic nervous system accelerates an action, the parasympathetic nervous system slows it. However, neither system is exclusively exitatory or inhibitory. The parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil, decrease the rate and force of heart beat, dilate many blood vessels, lower the arterial blood pressure, quicken the peristaltic movements, and contract the urinary bladder.

questions.text: GnRH a hypothalamic hormone, needed in reproduction acts on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH (Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone) is indeed a hypothalamic hormone involved in reproduction.It primarily acts on the anterior pituitary gland.GnRH stimulates the secretion of LH (Luteinizing Hormone) and FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) from the anterior pituitary gland.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of oxytocin and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH does not directly stimulate the posterior pituitary gland.FSH secretion is primarily regulated by GnRH and occurs in the anterior pituitary gland, not the posterior pituitary gland.Oxytocin is released from the posterior pituitary gland, but GnRH does not stimulate its secretion.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and relaxin
options.explanation: GnRH typically does not stimulate the posterior pituitary gland.Relaxin is not a hormone typically stimulated by GnRH.

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and oxytocin.
options.explanation: GnRH acts on the anterior pituitary gland.It stimulates the secretion of LH and FSH, not oxytocin.

questions.explanation: Gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH) is secreted by the hypothalamus which stimulates the anterior lobe of pituitary gland to secrete luteinising hormone (LH) and Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH).

questions.text: Hypersecretion of growth hormone in adults does not cause further increase in height because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Epiphyseal plates are areas of cartilage at the ends of long bones where growth occurs. They typically close or ossify after adolescence, which means they turn into bone and no longer allow for further increase in height.

options.text: Bones loose their sensitivity to growth hormone in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While it's true that the growth plates close after adolescence, it's not because bones lose their sensitivity to growth hormone. Instead, it's due to the natural developmental process.

options.text: Muscle fibres do not grow in size after birth
options.explanation: This statement is generally true. Muscle fibers can grow in size through hypertrophy, but this process is not primarily regulated by growth hormone. It's not directly related to height increase or the lack thereof.

options.text: Growth hormone becomes inactive in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Growth hormone remains active in adults, although its role shifts from primarily promoting longitudinal bone growth (height) during childhood and adolescence to other functions in adulthood, such as maintaining muscle mass and bone density.

questions.explanation: Epiphyseal plate is a hyaline cartilage plate in the metaphysis at each end of long bone. It is part of long bone where new bone growth takes place. In adults, elevated levels of GH results in acromegaly where no increase in height occurs because of ossified epiphyseal plate.

questions.text: A temporary endocrine gland in the human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The corpus cardiacum is a structure found in insects, not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is responsible for storing and releasing neurohormones.

The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in the human body. It forms in the ovary after ovulation when a mature follicle transforms into a structure that produces hormones, including progesterone, to support early pregnancy. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down.
The corpus allatum is also a structure found in insects and not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is involved in producing juvenile hormones that influence insect development.

The pineal gland is a small endocrine gland in the human brain. While it's not typically described as "temporary," it plays a crucial role in regulating the sleep-wake cycle and producing the hormone melatonin, which is involved in regulating sleep and circadian rhythms.

questions.explanation: Corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in human females. It secretes progesterone which stimulates the uterine glands to produce increased amount of watery mucus and is also essential for maintenance of endothelium. In absence of fertilisation, corpus luteum disintegrates leading to menstruation.

questions.text: Graves’ disease is caused due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyposecretion means the thyroid gland is not producing enough thyroid hormones.Graves' disease is actually characterized by hypersecretion (overproduction) of thyroid hormones, not hyposecretion.

This is the correct answer. Graves' disease is caused by an autoimmune response where the immune system stimulates the thyroid gland to produce excessive amounts of thyroid hormones, leading to hyperthyroidism.
Adrenal gland hyposecretion is unrelated to Graves' disease. The adrenal glands primarily produce hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, not thyroid hormones.

Adrenal gland hypersecretion is also unrelated to Graves' disease. An overactive adrenal gland might result in excessive production of adrenal hormones, but this condition is distinct from Graves' disease.

questions.explanation: Exophthalmic goitre or Graves’ disease is a thyroid enlargement (goitre) in which the thyroid secretes excessive amount of thyroid hormone. It is characterised by exophthalmia (protrusion of eye balls because of fluid accumulation behind them), loss of weight, slight rise in the body temperature, excitability, rapid heart beat, nervousness and restlessness.

questions.text: Name a peptide hormone which acts mainly on hepatocytes,adipocytes and enhances cellular glucose uptake and utilisation:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is produced by the pancreas and plays a crucial role in regulating blood sugar levels. It promotes glucose uptake by cells, including hepatocytes (liver cells) and adipocytes (fat cells), facilitating glucose utilization and storage.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. It raises blood sugar levels by promoting the release of glucose from the liver. It does not enhance cellular glucose uptake.

Secretin is a hormone produced by the small intestine that primarily stimulates the pancreas to release bicarbonate, aiding in neutralizing stomach acid. It does not have a direct role in glucose uptake or utilization.
Gastrin is produced by the stomach and plays a role in regulating stomach acid secretion. It is not involved in glucose metabolism or cellular glucose uptake.

questions.explanation: Insulin is a peptide hormone, secreted by the b cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas, that promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, particularly in the liver (hepatocytes) and muscles (adipocytes), and thereby controls its concentration in the blood.

questions.text: The posterior pituitary gland is not a ‘true’ endocrine gland because :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is provided with a duct
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The posterior pituitary gland is not a true endocrine gland because it does not have its own duct. Instead, it releases hormones directly into the bloodstream.

options.text: It only stores and releases hormones
options.explanation: This statement is also correct. The posterior pituitary gland primarily stores and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, such as oxytocin and vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone). It does not produce hormones on its own.

options.text: It is under the regulation of hypothalamus
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. The posterior pituitary gland is controlled by the hypothalamus, which produces and sends hormones to the posterior pituitary for storage and release. The hypothalamus acts as a control center for the release of these hormones.
This statement is incorrect. The posterior pituitary gland does not secrete enzymes; its main function is to store and release hormones.

questions.explanation: Posterior lobe of pituitary gland does not secrete any hormone. Its hormones are synthesised by the hypothalamus. It only stores and releases these hormones. Hence, it cannot be considered as true gland.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas, and it plays a crucial role in sugar metabolism. Its main function is to lower blood sugar levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells, where it can be used for energy or stored as glycogen.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. Glucagon raises blood sugar levels by promoting the breakdown of glycogen into glucose in the liver and stimulating the release of glucose into the bloodstream.

Cortisone is a corticosteroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands. While cortisone does have various metabolic effects, it primarily regulates inflammation and has a relatively minor role in sugar metabolism compared to insulin and glucagon.

Aldosterone is also a hormone produced by the adrenal glands, but it primarily regulates salt and water balance in the body, particularly in the kidneys. It does not have a direct role in sugar metabolism.

questions.explanation: Aldosterone (saltretaining hormone) is the principal mineralocorticoid in humans, secreted by adrenal cortex. Its main function is to regulate sodium content of the body.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is released by the posterior part of pituitary gland?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prolactin is not released by the posterior pituitary but by the anterior pituitary gland. It primarily stimulates milk production in the mammary glands.
MSH is produced by cells in the anterior pituitary and not the posterior pituitary. It plays a role in regulating skin pigmentation.

This is the correct answer. ADH is produced by the neurons in the posterior pituitary. It regulates water balance in the body by influencing the reabsorption of water in the kidneys, thereby reducing urine production and helping to maintain blood pressure.

LH is also released by the anterior pituitary, not the posterior pituitary. In males, LH stimulates the production of testosterone, and in females, it triggers ovulation and supports the formation of the corpus luteum.

questions.explanation: Two hormones, i.e oxytocin (OT) and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) are synthesised in the hypothalamus, but stored and released by the posterior lobe of pituitary gland. Hypothalamus is regarded as the Master Gland.

questions.text: A pregnant female delivers a baby who suffers from stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient and abnormal skin.This is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thyroid cancer typically leads to an overproduction of thyroid hormones rather than a deficiency. Therefore, it is not likely to be the cause of the described symptoms.

The pars distalis is a part of the anterior pituitary gland that secretes various hormones, but it does not produce thyroid hormones directly. Oversecretion of hormones from the pars distalis could lead to other issues but is not directly related to the symptoms described.

This is the correct answer. Iodine is an essential nutrient required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones (T3 and T4). A deficiency of iodine in the diet can lead to hypothyroidism, particularly in pregnant women, which can result in the described symptoms in the newborn. This condition is known as congenital hypothyroidism or cretinism.

Growth hormone deficiency can result in stunted growth, but it is not typically associated with the specific combination of symptoms mentioned, such as mental retardation, low intelligence quotient, and abnormal skin.

questions.explanation: Iodine is needed for the synthesis of T3 and T4. Iodine binds to the tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin, which is then hydrolysed into iodotyrosines that combine to form tri-iodothyronine (T3 ) or thyroxine (tetraiodothyronine or T4 ). Therefore, deficiency of iodine in the diet of a pregnant female will lead to the improper synthesis of thyroid hormones in the newly borne infant.
hints: stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient

questions.text: Norepinephrine (i)is released by sympathetic fibers (ii)is released by parasympathetic fibers (iii)increases the heart rate (iv)decreases blood pressureWhich of the above statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sympathetic fibers are also called the small fiber nerves. It is developed by the collections of nerve clusters called ganglia. It is available on the outside of the spinal cord. It will increase the rate of heart beat, muscle contraction and rate of conduction. So, i is correct. And iii is also correct.

The nerve fibers of the parasympathetic fibers nervous system are cranial nerves, vagus nerve and lumber of spinal nerve. During this nerve stimulation it increases the digestive secretion and also reduces the heartbeat. Norepinephrine hormone will increase the heart beat. So, ii is not correct and iii is correct.
Sympathetic fibers release the norepinephrine. Blood pressure is increased during the large amount of norepinephrine hormone excretion. So, i is correct but iv is not the correct answer.
Parasympathetic fibers do not produce the norepinephrine hormone. Decreasing blood pressure is not possible during the excretion of hormones. So, both ii and iv are not correct.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is secreted by some neurons of the sympathetic nervous system and also by adrenal medulla. It accelerates heart rate.

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs of hormones are the examples of those that can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it(mostly in the nucleus)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin has the receptor on the outer surface of cells which let them cross the barrier easily
Insulin has the receptor on the outer surface of cells which let them cross the barrier easily
Oxytocin has its receptors on myometrium of uterus.

Steroid hormones such as cortisol, testosterone, estradiol and progesterone, mostly regulate gene expression or chromosome function by the interaction of the hormone-receptor complex with the genome. So, these can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones such as cortisol, testosterone, estradiol and progesterone, mostly regulate gene expression or chromosome function by the interaction of the hormone-receptor complex with the genome. So, these can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it.

questions.text: Low Ca+ in the body fluid may be the cause of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Low levels of blood Ca++ ions stimulate the parathyroid directly to increase parathormone production whereas high evels of Ca++ ions suppress its release. Under-activity causes a drop in blood Ca++ ions which in turn leads to muscular tetany.
Angina pectoris—or simply angina—is chest pain or discomfort that keeps coming back.

Gout is a common and complex form of arthritis that can affect anyone. It's characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain, swelling, redness and tenderness in one or more joints, most often in the big toe.

questions.explanation: Tetany is a spasm and twitching of the muscles, particularly those of the face, hands, and feet. Tetany is usually caused by a reduction in the blood calcium level, which may be due to underactive parathyroid glands, rickets, or alkalosis.

questions.text: The blood calcium level is lowered by the deficiency of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parathormone and calcitonin are antagonistic to each other.

A lack of calcitonin in the blood may increase a person's risk of bone degradation and osteopenia, a condition that occurs when the rate in which the body reabsorbs old bone exceeds the rate in which it produces new bone.
Hypoparathyroidism occurs when the glands produce too little PTH. The blood calcium level falls, and the phosphorus level rises.
In adults, thyroxine deficiency will lower the metabolic rate, causing weight gain, memory problems, infertility, fatigue, and muscle stiffness.

questions.explanation: Parathormone is secreted by chief cells of the parathyroid that regulate the metabolism of calcium and phosphate. It increases calcium absorption from the intestine and also increases calcium resorption from the nephrons of the kidneys. So its deficiency leads to low blood calcium level.

questions.text: A person is having problems with calcium and phosphorus metabolism in his body. Which one of the following glands may not be functioning properly?

This is the first section of the small intestine. The main hormones secreted by the endocrine gland in the pancreas are insulin and glucagon, which regulate the level of glucose in the blood, and somatostatin, which prevents the release of insulin and glucagon.
The thyroid gland produces hormones that regulate the body's metabolic rate, growth and development.

Parathormone is secreted by chief cells of the parathyroid that regulates the metabolism of calcium and phosphate. It increases calcium absorption from the intestine and also increases calcium resorption from the nephrons of the kidneys. So its deficiency leads to low blood calcium level.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs correctly matches a hormone with a disease resulting from its deficiency?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: LH induces ovulation therefore, decrease in LH will result in the failure of ovulation.
Without enough insulin, extra glucose stays in your bloodstream rather than entering your cells. Over time, you could develop type 2 diabetes. i.e: Diabetes Insipidus.

A high TSH and low thyroid hormone level (e.g. low FT4) can indicate primary hypothyroidism. Primary hypothyroidism occurs when the thyroid gland makes too little thyroid hormone. Symptoms of hypothyroidism can include feeling cold, constipation, weight gain, slowed thinking, and decreased energy. Tetany is caused by low Calcium levels in blood.

Hypoparathyroidism occurs when the glands produce too little PTH. The blood calcium level falls, and the phosphorus level rises. The most common cause of hypoparathyroidism is injury to the parathyroid glands during thyroid or neck surgery.

questions.explanation: Ovulation occurs under the influence of the luteinizing hormone and FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) of the anterior pituitary gland. Thus, deficiency of luteinizing hormone results in failure of ovulation.

questions.text: Chemically, hormones are _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There are five known biogenic amines: dopamine, epinephrine, norepinephrine, histamine and serotonin, which are formed by the decarboxylation of amino acids or amination and transamination of aldehydes and ketones during standard metabolic processes.

Hormones are chemical messengers produced by the ductless glands (sometimes by neurons) and transported in the circulation to target cells. They regulate metabolic processes. Chemically, hormones are of different nature like biogenic amines (like thyroxine, adrenaline etc), proteinaceous or polypeptides (like hypothalamic hormones etc.) and steroids (like sex hormones and adrenocorticoids).
Hormones are made up of protein like insulin, glucagon etc
There are steroid hormones like aldosteron, cortisone etc.

questions.text: Acromegaly is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acromegaly is a condition caused by an excess of growth hormone, which causes the overgrowth of bones in the face, hands and feet. A tumour on the pituitary gland is the most common cause of acromegaly.
In hyperthyroidism, the thyroid gland produces too much thyroxine or triiodothyronine, which speeds up the body's metabolism

questions.text: Mainly which type of hormones control the menstrual cycle in human beings?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The menstrual cycle is controlled by several endocrinal parameters. In the beginning of the cycle, FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) of the pituitary initiates the development of an ovarian follicle. A growing ovarian follicle gradually secretes an increasing amount of estrogen. This in turn leads to a sudden surge of LH secretion by the pituitary. As the LH (luteinising hormone) level in blood suddenly increases there is ovulation. Thus only FSH or LH cannot control all the events of the menstrual cycle. Progesterone is released by the corpus luteum after ovulation which actually prepares the uterus for a possible pregnancy. If there is no fertilisation, progesterone level falls and there is the beginning of a new cycle.

questions.text: “Law of independent assortment” states:

options.text: That each pair of alleles assort independently of other pairs of alleles during gamete formation

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.text: The genotype of blood group 'AB' in humans is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the human blood group system, there are four main types: A, B, AB, and O. Each individual inherits two alleles for the ABO blood group, one from each parent. The A and B alleles are codominant, meaning that both are expressed when they are both present in an individual's genotype. The O allele is recessive, so it is only expressed when an individual has two copies of the O allele. The genotype of blood group AB is IAIB, which means that the individual has inherited one A allele and one B allele from their parents. These two alleles are codominant, so both the A and B antigens are expressed on the surface of their red blood cells. Individuals with blood group AB are considered universal recipients because they can receive blood from individuals with any blood group without causing a transfusion reaction. However, they can only donate blood to individuals with blood group AB.
This genotype represents blood group B, as it has the B allele from one parent and an O allele from the other parent.

questions.text: The total number of genes present in a particular population, at a particular time is called:

options.explanation: A gene is a specific segment of DNA that contains the instructions for building a particular protein or determining a specific trait. However, the total number of genes present in a population is not referred to as a "gene."

The genome refers to the complete set of genetic material (DNA) present in an organism. It includes all the genes, non-coding sequences, and regulatory elements. The genome encompasses the entire genetic information of an individual or a species.

Genotype refers to the genetic constitution of an individual, specifically the combination of alleles present in an organism's DNA. It represents the genetic information that determines the observable traits (phenotype) of an organism. However, the term "genotype" does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

Gene pool refers to the sum total of all the genes, alleles, and their variations present in a population at a particular time. It includes all the genetic information and diversity available within a population. The gene pool of a population is subject to change due to various factors such as mutation, migration, genetic drift, and natural selection.

A karyotype is a visual representation of the chromosomes of an individual or a species, arranged in a specific order. It allows for examining the number, structure, and size of chromosomes. While a karyotype provides valuable information about the organization of chromosomes, it does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

questions.text: Which light photon has the least momentum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light, about 700 nanometers. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum. Therefore, the momentum of a red photon is the least of all visible light photons.
Green light has a wavelength of about 550 nanometers. This is shorter than the wavelength of red light, so the momentum of a green photon is greater than the momentum of a red photon.

Yellow light has a wavelength of about 580 nanometers. This is between the wavelengths of red and green light, so the momentum of a yellow photon is between the momentum of a red photon and the momentum of a green photon.
Blue light has a wavelength of about 400 nanometers. This is the shortest wavelength of all visible light, so the momentum of a blue photon is the greatest of all visible light photons.

questions.explanation: The light photon with the least momentum is red. This is because red light has the longest wavelength of all visible light. The momentum of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, so the photon with the longest wavelength will have the least momentum. The momentum of a photon can be calculated using the following equation: p = h/λ where p is the momentum, h is Planck's constant, and λ is the wavelength. The wavelength of red light is about 700 nanometers, while the wavelength of blue light is about 400 nanometers. This means that the momentum of a red photon is about half the momentum of a blue photon. Therefore, the light photon with the least momentum is red.

questions.text: The number of photoelectrons emitted from metal depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The intensity of incident light refers to the brightness or the amount of light energy falling on the metal surface per unit area per unit time. According to the photoelectric effect, the number of photoelectrons emitted from a metal surface depends directly on the intensity of the incident light. Higher-intensity light means more photons, thus more photoelectrons are emitted. However, the energy of individual photons remains the same.

The energy of the incident light (i.e., the energy carried by each photon) plays a significant role in the photoelectric effect. For photoelectrons to be emitted from the metal, the energy of the incident photons must be equal to or greater than the work function of the metal (the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal's surface). Only photons with sufficient energy can cause photoemission.

The wavelength of the incident light is directly related to its energy. Shorter wavelengths (higher energy photons) have a greater chance of causing photoemission compared to longer wavelengths with lower energies. The energy of a photon is directly proportional to the frequency of light and inversely proportional to its wavelength.

The frequency of the incident light determines the energy of the individual photons. Photons with higher frequencies (corresponding to shorter wavelengths) have more energy and are more likely to cause the emission of photoelectrons.

The galvanometer will show no current when a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that the maximum lines of magnetic force pass through the coil. When the magnet is stationary and its magnetic field is not changing, there will be no induced electromotive force (EMF) and therefore no induced current in the coil. The galvanometer will show no current. For induced current to flow, there must be a changing magnetic field. If you were to move the magnet relative to the coil, such as by sliding it back and forth, or rotating it, the magnetic field passing through the coil would change, and this changing magnetic field would induce an EMF and a corresponding induced current in the coil. The magnitude of the induced current would depend on factors such as the rate of change of the magnetic field and the properties of the coil.

When the magnet is stationary and its magnetic field is not changing, there will be no induced electromotive force (EMF) and therefore no induced current in the coil. The galvanometer will show no current. For induced current to flow, there must be a changing magnetic field. If you were to move the magnet relative to the coil, such as by sliding it back and forth, or rotating it, the magnetic field passing through the coil would change, and this changing magnetic field would induce an EMF and a corresponding induced current in the coil. The magnitude of the induced current would depend on factors such as the rate of change of the magnetic field and the properties of the coil.

questions.text: The SI unit of magnetic flux is Weber which is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The unit NA/m is the unit of magnetic field strength, also known as magnetic field intensity or magnetic field intensity. It represents the SI unit for magnetic field strength and is measured in amperes per meter (A/m).

The unit NmA is not a recognized unit in the SI system. Newton-meter (Nm) is the SI unit of torque or moment of force, representing the product of a force (measured in newtons) and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (measured in meters). Milliampere (mA) is the SI unit for electrical current, representing one thousandth of an ampere. Combining the two units, NmA, would not have a specific meaning or representation in a recognized SI unit.

The unit Nm^-1A^-1 represents the reciprocal of the unit for electrical resistance, which is ohm (Ω). Reciprocal of ohm: (Ω)^-1 = Ω^-1 = Nm^-1A^-1 Therefore, Nm^-1A^-1 is the unit of electrical conductance, which is the measure of how easily electric current can flow through a material. It is commonly denoted by the unit siemens (S) in the SI system.

The SI unit of magnetic flux is indeed the Weber (Wb). The Weber is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that passes through a surface of one square meter (1 m²) perpendicular to a magnetic field of one Tesla (1 T). In terms of base SI units, one Weber is equivalent to one Tesla meter squared (T·m²).

questions.explanation: The bond length for a given pair of elements is a function of number and types of orbital overlaps. Single bonds, with their end-to-end overlap called sigma bonds are the longest (and also the weakest). A double bond consists of a sigma bond and a pi bond, the side to side overlap of p-orbitals. This moves the nuclei closer together and decreases the bond length (and increases the bond energy). A triple bond consists of two pi bonds and a sigma bond. The additional pi bond isn’t any shorter than the first pi bond, but together the two pi bonds provide additional attraction and shorten the sigma bond allowing the triple bond to be the shortest (and strongest).C−C ………. 154 pmC=C ………. 134 pmC≡C ………. 121 pm

To convert an alkyne into a cis alkene, the reagent commonly used is Lindlar's catalyst. Lindlar's catalyst consists of palladium (Pd) deposited on calcium carbonate (CaCO3) or barium sulfate (BaSO4) support, with quinoline or lead acetate as a poison to prevent further reduction to an alkane. Lindlar's catalyst selectively reduces the triple bond of an alkyne to a cis-alkene or trans-alkene, depending on the reaction conditions. By using Lindlar's catalyst under appropriate conditions (such as low temperature and low hydrogen pressure), cis-alkenes can be obtained. The reaction mechanism involves the partial reduction of the alkyne, stopping at the alkene stage. The presence of the poison helps to hinder the complete reduction to an alkane and directs the selectivity towards the cis-alkene product. Therefore, Lindlar's catalyst is the reagent commonly employed to convert an alkyne into a cis alkene.

The general formula for the aldehyde group is R-CHO or R-COH. The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde with the formula HCOH. Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3).An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. All other aldehydes have one hydrogen bonded to the carbonyl group, like the simple molecule acetaldehyde, which has one hydrogen and one methyl group (HCOCH3).

questions.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3). An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. All other aldehydes have one hydrogen bonded to the carbonyl group, like the simple molecule acetaldehyde, which has one hydrogen and one methyl group (HCOCH3). The carbonyl carbon in both aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic, meaning that it has a dipole due to the electronegativity of the attached oxygen atom. This makes the carbonyl carbon an ideal target for nucleophiles in a nucleophilic addition reaction. During this reaction, the nucleophile, or electron donor, attacks the carbonyl to form the tetrahedral intermediate. The negatively charged oxygen accepts a hydrogen ion to form a hydroxyl group. Typically, nucleophiles possess a negative charge or lone pair on a heteroatom, which can take several forms (OH-, RO-, CN-, R3C-, RNH2, ROH). For primary amines (RNH2), the reaction does not stop at the formation of the tetrahedral intermediate with a hydroxyl group. Rather, an elimination reaction occurs that produces a double-bonded carbon and nitrogen functional group known as an imine. Understanding the reactions that aldehydes and ketones can undergo provides a way to differentiate between these similar organic compounds.

questions.text: By treating CO2 with Grignard’s reagent, the following compound is obtained;

options.explanation: Treating CO2 with Grignard's reagent does not typically result in the formation of aromatic compounds. The Grignard reaction is not commonly used for the synthesis of aromatic compounds.

Treating CO2 with Grignard's reagent does not directly lead to the formation of alcohols. While Grignard reagents can react with carbonyl compounds (such as aldehydes or ketones) to form alcohols, the reaction with CO2 results in the formation of carboxylic acids, not alcohols.

When carbon dioxide (CO₂) reacts with a Grignard reagent (RMgX), a carboxylic acid is formed. The Grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbon in CO₂. After the addition of the Grignard reagent, the intermediate is hydrolyzed, resulting in the formation of a carboxylic acid and a magnesium salt.

Treating CO2 with Grignard's reagent does not lead to the formation of ethers. Ethers are typically synthesized through other methods, such as the Williamson ether synthesis or the dehydration of alcohols.

questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Propanoic acid (propionic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.
Fluoroethanoic acid (fluoroacetic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.

Among the given options, trichloroethanoic acid is the strongest acid.The strength of an acid is determined by its ability to donate protons (H+ ions) in an aqueous solution. Acids with more electronegative atoms or more electron-withdrawing groups tend to be stronger acids because they stabilize the negative charge on the conjugate base, making it easier to release a proton.Trichloroethanoic acid (also known as trichloroacetic acid) has three chlorine atoms attached to the acetic acid (ethanoic acid) backbone. The chlorine atoms are highly electronegative, which stabilizes the negative charge on the conjugate base (trichloroacetate ion) and facilitates the release of a proton, making it a strong acid. The other options are weaker acids compared to trichloroethanoic acid:Propanoic acid (propionic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.Fluoroethanoic acid (fluoroacetic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.Nitroethanoic acid (nitroacetic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.So, trichloroethanoic acid is the strongest acid among the given options.
Nitroethanoic acid (nitroacetic acid) is weaker than trichloroethanoic acid.

questions.explanation: Trichloroacetic Acid (TCA) is a much stronger acid than all the acids mentioned in the options, from a chemical ionization standpoint, because the electronegative Chlorine atoms draw electron density away from the carboxyl end of the molecule, creating a partial positive charge on the carboxyl group, and allowing easier removal of the positively charged Hydrogen ion, H+.

questions.text: The color of transition metal complexes is due to transition of electrons between:

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct word. The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it _ ever ploughed.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence is referring to a past event, indicating that the shepherd ploughed the mountain with cattle for the first time. The use of the past tense "was" is appropriate to describe the action that occurred in the past. Therefore, the correct sentence is: "The shephard ploughed this mountain with cattle for the first time it was ever ploughed."

The phrase "was been" is incorrect in this blank because it represents a redundant and grammatically incorrect use of verb forms."Was" and "been" are both past tense forms of the verb "to be," but they cannot be used together in a sentence. The correct forms are either "was" or "been," depending on the context.Here's a breakdown of the correct usage:"Was" is the simple past tense of the verb "to be" and is used when describing an action that happened in the past, without any specific relation to the present. For example: "He was happy yesterday.""Been" is the past participle of the verb "to be" and is used in combination with other verbs to indicate actions that started in the past and continue into the present or have completed at some point before the present. For example: "He has been to London."In the given sentence, "the first time it was ever ploughed," the use of "was" correctly indicates the past action of the mountain being ploughed for the first time. Adding "been" after "was" would create a grammatically incorrect structure, as "been" is not required in this context.Corrected sentence: The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it was ever ploughed.

The word "had" is incorrect in this blank because it represents the past perfect tense, which is not appropriate for this context.The past perfect tense is used to describe an action that occurred before another action in the past. It is formed by combining the past tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb. For example: "He had already eaten before I arrived."In the original sentence, we are not describing an action that occurred before another past action. Instead, we are simply stating that the mountain was ploughed for the first time. There is no need to introduce a past perfect construction here because there is no other past action in the sentence that the ploughing happened before.

The phrase "had been" is the past perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an action that started in the past, continued for some time, and then stopped before another past event. It is formed by combining the past perfect tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb (in this case, "been").
